figures.caption: Table 3: Results for the third simulation experiment, evaluating double robustness. The experiment is run on observational scenarios (OBS-1, 2, 3) with n = 5 × 105 and r = 5000. Performance metrics (RMSE, CI Coverage, CI Width) are compared for UD-DML (UD) and UNIF-DML (UNIF) under four nuisance model specifications: (Correct, Correct), (Correct, Wrong), (Wrong, Correct), and (Wrong, Wrong) for the outcome (m) and propensity (e) models, respectively.

Table 2: Simulation results for the scalability experiment. Performance is evaluated as the full data size n increases from 105 to 5 × 105 while the subsample size r remains fixed at two levels (r = 1000 and r = 5000). The table reports four key metrics: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), Coverage of 95% Confidence Interval (CI Coverage), CI Width, and wall-clock Runtime in seconds. Results compare our proposed UD-DML (UD) method against the benchmark UNIF-DML (UNIF) and the DML estimator computed on the full dataset (FULL).

Figure 6: Bootstrap behaviour of UD-DML and UNIF-DML at the subsample size r = 5000 on the CDC 2021 natality data (n = 2,846,543, B = 100 replications). Left: bootstrap distribution of θ̂sub ∈ {θ̂UD, θ̂UNIF}; the dashed horizontal line is the FULL-DML reference θ̂FULL and the shaded band is its 95% Wald confidence interval. Centre: empirical cumulative distribution function of the absolute deviation |θ̂sub− θ̂FULL| on a logarithmic horizontal axis. Right: mean wall-clock speed-up relative to FULL-DML on the entire sample.

Figure 7: Subsample-size scaling on the CDC 2021 natality data. Each panel reports a bootstrap-averaged metric across the sample size grid r ∈ {1000, 2500, 5000, 10000, 25000} for UD-DML (orange) and UNIF-DML (blue). Left: root mean squared error of the subsample estimator relative to the FULL-DML reference. Centre: bootstrap Monte-Carlo standard deviation, which captures the rep-to-rep dispersion of the estimator. Right: mean wall-clock runtime per call, with the dashed horizontal line marking the FULL-DML runtime on the full sample. All panels use logarithmic axes.

Figure 4: Q–Q plot of the standardised UD-DML estimator on OBS-3 (n = 5× 105, r = 5000, B = 500). The empirical quantiles closely track the 45◦ reference line, supporting the √ rasymptotic normality established in Theorem 3.

Figure 5: Love plot of covariate-wise standardised mean differences (SMD) between the treated and control groups in OBS-3. The UD-selected subsample concentrates SMDs tightly around zero, whereas the UNIF-selected subsample reproduces the imbalance of the full sample, mirroring the balance guarantee of Theorem 2.

Figure 2: Propensity score distributions for the three observational study (OBS) scenarios. We present the distributions from Full data, subdata selected by uniform design (UD) and the uniform subsampling (UNIF). The scenarios are designed with varying levels of overlap: OBS-1 (left) features high overlap, OBS-2 (center) has moderate overlap, and OBS-3 (right) presents a challenging low-overlap case. The blue histograms represent the control group (W = 0) and the red histograms represent the treated group (W = 1).
Figure 3: Overlap-gradient experiment on the OBS-3 variant with tunable propensity multiplier c ∈ {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5} at n = 5× 105, r = 5000.

Figure 1: Results for the first simulation experiment, assessing statistical efficiency as a function of subsample size r. Plots show Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), Coverage of 95% Confidence Interval (CI Coverage), and CI Width for UD-DML (orange) and UNIF-DML (blue) across the three observational DGPs. The full data size is fixed at n = 5× 105 while r varies from 1000 to 10000.
